conclusions.summary: The Lehmer hard gap [1.176281, 1.240726] (width 0.064446) is structural: zero NF defining polynomials fall in it across 705K sampled. Lehmer compositions at degrees 20, 40 achieve M = Lehmer exactly. The minimum non-composition measure is 1.240726 at degree 12. In the broader Salem number landscape (Boyd's table), values do approach Lehmer from above, suggesting it is an accumulation point of Salem numbers — but the LMFDB NF polynomials are too sparse at high degree to see this accumulation. The gap is consistent with Lehmer's conjecture: M = 1.176... is the true infimum for non-cyclotomic algebraic integers.
conclusions.trinomial_floor_dominance: From degree 22 onward, the minimum non-cyclotomic Mahler measure in LMFDB equals M(x^n - x - 1) exactly. This trinomial family converges to M_inf ~= 1.38136 with even/odd oscillation. The floor is set by the simplest possible non-trivial polynomial family.

three_regimes.regime_1: Degrees 2-6: Floor set by algebraic integers of specific forms (quadratics, cubics), M >> 1.5
three_regimes.regime_2: Degrees 7-21: Mixed floor — some degrees match x^n - x - 1, others have smaller Salem numbers (e.g. degree 12: M=1.241, degree 10: M=1.284)
three_regimes.regime_3: Degrees 22-47: Floor EXACTLY equals M(x^n - x - 1), converging to 1.38136

gap_structure.soft_gap: [1.24073, 1.38136) — sparsely populated, contains degree-12 minimum and a few others
gap_structure.trinomial_floor: 1.38136 — asymptotic floor for generic (non-Salem, non-special) polynomials
gap_structure.lehmer_isolation: Lehmer constant is separated from the trinomial floor by gap = 0.205 (18% of Lehmer value)

conclusions.verdict: Lehmer constant 1.17628 is an ISOLATED MINIMUM within LMFDB number field polynomials. The asymptotic floor (1.38136 from trinomial family) is 0.205 above Lehmer. Only Lehmer polynomial compositions (degree 20, 40, 60...) achieve the Lehmer value. Boyd table Salem numbers (1.188-1.233) would fill part of the gap but are absent from LMFDB. The gap is STRUCTURAL: degree does not close it. Higher degree just converges to the trinomial floor. This is SHARPER than expected — the gap does NOT soften with degree.

trinomial_floor.description: The per-degree minimum Mahler measure in LMFDB exactly matches M(x^n - x - 1) for all degrees >= 22
trinomial_floor.match_count: 29/41 for degrees 7-47 (all misses are degrees with smaller non-trinomial polys)
